title: Logs are missing in remote attach STDOUT with Logs var set to true with k8s-file log driver
body:
### Issue Description

Sometimes when using `k8s-file` log driver and trying to remotely attach to the running container with `Logs` option set to `true` in `containers.AttachOptions`  some logs are missing. Usually it happens when the machine is under load, it also happens without any load but quite rarely. 

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. I managed to somehow reliably reproduce the issue by running this code snippet :
 
```
package main

import (
	"bytes"
	"context"
	"errors"
	"fmt"
	"log"
	"os"
	"strings"
	"time"

	"github.com/containers/podman/v5/libpod/define"
	"github.com/containers/podman/v5/pkg/bindings"
	"github.com/containers/podman/v5/pkg/bindings/containers"
	"github.com/containers/podman/v5/pkg/specgen"
)

const (
	logFieName = "test.log"

	missingLog = "I am a missing log"
)

var (
	trueVar                = true
	containerRemoveTimeout = uint(1)
)

func main() {
	connection, err := bindings.NewConnection(context.Background(), "unix:///run/user/1000/podman/podman.sock")
	if err != nil {
		log.Fatal(err)
	}

	dir, err := os.Getwd()
	if err != nil {
		log.Fatal(err)
	}
	logFilePath := dir + "/" + logFieName

	cnt := 0
	for {
		func() {
			cnt++
			log.Println("Iteration: ", cnt)

			err = os.Remove(logFilePath)
			if err != nil && !errors.Is(err, os.ErrNotExist) {
				log.Fatal(err)
			}

			containerCreateResponse, err := containers.CreateWithSpec(connection, &specgen.SpecGenerator{
				ContainerBasicConfig: specgen.ContainerBasicConfig{
					Name:     "test-log",
					Command:  []string{"/bin/sh", "-c", fmt.Sprintf("echo '%s' && /bin/sh", missingLog)},
					Stdin:    &trueVar,
					Terminal: &trueVar,
					LogConfiguration: &specgen.LogConfig{
						Driver: "k8s-file",
						Path:   logFilePath,
						Size:   4096,
					},
				},
				ContainerStorageConfig: specgen.ContainerStorageConfig{
					Image: "alpine",
				},

				ContainerHealthCheckConfig: specgen.ContainerHealthCheckConfig{
					HealthLogDestination: define.DefaultHealthCheckLocalDestination,
					HealthMaxLogCount:    define.DefaultHealthMaxLogCount,
					HealthMaxLogSize:     define.DefaultHealthMaxLogSize,
				},
			}, &containers.CreateOptions{})
			if err != nil {
				log.Fatal(err)
			}

			defer func() {
				_, err = containers.Remove(connection, containerCreateResponse.ID, &containers.RemoveOptions{
					Force:   &trueVar,
					Ignore:  &trueVar,
					Timeout: &containerRemoveTimeout,
				})
				if err != nil {
					log.Fatal(err)
				}
			}()

			err = containers.Start(connection, containerCreateResponse.ID, &containers.StartOptions{})
			if err != nil {
				log.Fatal(err)
			}

			attachReady := make(chan bool)
			buf := bytes.Buffer{}
			go func() {
				err := containers.Attach(connection, containerCreateResponse.ID, os.Stdin, &buf, &buf, attachReady, &containers.AttachOptions{Logs: &trueVar})
				if err != nil {
					log.Fatal(err)
				}
			}()
			<-attachReady

			shellPromptMissing := true
			for i := 0; i < 100; i++ {
				if strings.Contains(buf.String(), "/ #") {
					shellPromptMissing = false
					break
				}
				time.Sleep(20 * time.Millisecond)
				continue
			}

			if shellPromptMissing {
				log.Println("Shell prompt missing")
				return
			}

			if !strings.Contains(buf.String(), missingLog) {
				log.Fatalf("Log is missing, actual STDOUT: %s", buf.String())
			}
		}()
	}
}

```

`go run main.go`

2.  I also had to simulate the load, I did it with `stress-ng --cpu=0  --vm 4 --vm-bytes 98% --timeout 600s`

### Describe the results you received

These are the results I have recieved:  

```
$ go run main.go 
2025/09/01 15:58:21 Iteration:  1
2025/09/01 15:58:22 Iteration:  2
2025/09/01 15:58:24 Iteration:  3
2025/09/01 15:58:28 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:58:28 Iteration:  4
2025/09/01 15:58:32 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:58:32 Iteration:  5
2025/09/01 15:58:36 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:58:36 Iteration:  6
2025/09/01 15:58:38 Iteration:  7
2025/09/01 15:58:42 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:58:42 Iteration:  8
2025/09/01 15:58:46 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:58:46 Iteration:  9
2025/09/01 15:58:50 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:58:50 Iteration:  10
2025/09/01 15:58:54 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:58:54 Iteration:  11
2025/09/01 15:58:58 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:58:58 Iteration:  12
2025/09/01 15:59:02 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:02 Iteration:  13
2025/09/01 15:59:04 Iteration:  14
2025/09/01 15:59:08 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:08 Iteration:  15
2025/09/01 15:59:12 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:12 Iteration:  16

2025/09/01 15:59:16 Iteration:  17
2025/09/01 15:59:20 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:20 Iteration:  18
2025/09/01 15:59:21 Iteration:  19
2025/09/01 15:59:25 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:25 Iteration:  20
2025/09/01 15:59:29 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:29 Iteration:  21
2025/09/01 15:59:34 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:34 Iteration:  22
2025/09/01 15:59:37 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:37 Iteration:  23
2025/09/01 15:59:41 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:41 Iteration:  24
2025/09/01 15:59:46 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:46 Iteration:  25
2025/09/01 15:59:50 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:50 Iteration:  26
2025/09/01 15:59:54 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:54 Iteration:  27
2025/09/01 15:59:58 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 15:59:58 Iteration:  28
2025/09/01 16:00:02 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 16:00:02 Iteration:  29
2025/09/01 16:00:04 Iteration:  30
2025/09/01 16:00:08 Shell prompt missing
2025/09/01 16:00:08 Iteration:  31
2025/09/01 16:00:10 Iteration:  32
2025/09/01 16:00:12 Log is missing, actual STDOUT: / # 
^[[49;56Rexit status 1
```

Actually there are two different outcomes: sometimes the shell promt does not appear at all (what is interesting is that it will not appear if the simulated load is removed) and sometimes a few logs before it are missing, as it happened during iteration 32.

In the `test.log` file all the logs are present 

```
2025-09-01T16:00:12.028984623+02:00 stdout F I am a missing log
2025-09-01T16:00:12.031859104+02:00 stdout P / # [6n
```

### Describe the results you expected

All the logs to be printed into STDOUT if the `Logs` option is set to true when using attach remote call

### podman info output

### Additional information

comments.body:
Not sure if it is related but there is https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/21914 which describes some problems with the file reading.

And just to be clear you don't see any problem with the journald driver?

Because looking the your reproducer I don't think your assumption can hold, attach (with the logs option set) reads the logs until the end of the logfile and then starts streaming the logs via the attach socket which is quite different from the regular log reading. Because to me it seems quite likely that we loose output between logs reading and attach which I would call expected.

I am not sure what you are doing but the race free[1] way to start and attach is doing this instead: https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/39072f7ce25fdc29c56f8fd47c5944c016bc00d4/pkg/domain/infra/tunnel/containers.go#L681-L755 and then you don't need the logs option at all.

> And just to be clear you don't see any problem with the journald driver?

I have not expirienced this issue with journald log driver so far.

> I am not sure what you are doing but the race free[1] way to start and attach is doing this instead:

What I am trying to do is to attach to the quadlet container. Since I cannot attach to container that does not exist, I rely on the message from the D-Bus to synchronize the attach call. With this approach I am only able to attach to the container that is already running. That is basically the reason why I was relying on the `Logs` option to recieve all the logs.
Can you build the server from source with https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/e1580ebea3892c5f6c14cd44e5dd55e037b7b37a and see if that improves anything.

> Can you build the server from source with https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/e1580ebea3892c5f6c14cd44e5dd55e037b7b37a and see if that improves anything.

Tried it out, no improvements

> I don't think your assumption can hold, attach (with the logs option set) reads the logs until the end of the logfile and then starts streaming the logs via the attach socket which is quite different from the regular log reading. 

That what I was expecting when I have read the comment https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/fcdb6b59d93a7b36e94648155747084e8a937575/pkg/bindings/containers/types.go#L47 next to the `Log` field at `AttachOptions`. There are no hints that might lead to conclusion that some logs might be lost because of the race condition.  

> Because to me it seems quite likely that we loose output between logs reading and attach which I would call expected.

I think this contradicts the above-mentioned comment from the source

I had a chance to debug the issue a bit more with the same setup and what I have found out, that all of the logs are read from the log file and then they are written to `httpBuf` here: https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/da88a5b0977f4387fc60fba00b42863226cdec3e/libpod/oci_conmon_common.go#L589
and then they are flushed without an error here: 
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/da88a5b0977f4387fc60fba00b42863226cdec3e/libpod/oci_conmon_common.go#L599
But one of the logs still is not delivered to the client side

Alright, after more debugging I could tell that the reason for race condition is the bug on the programmatic client side.  I have found out that the log arrives to the client together with the upgrade response from the server, I was able to verify it by   using `strace` and observing the read by the process that uses podman go client:
```
"HTTP/1.1 101 UPGRADED\r\nContent-Type: application/vnd.docker.raw-stream\r\nConnection: Upgrade\r\nUpgrade: tcp\r\n\r\nI am a missing log\r\n/ # \33[6n"
```
What seems to happen is that the two flush calls get merged into one, the original one that is being made right after hijacking the connection together with the next log flush. 

Here: 
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/da88a5b0977f4387fc60fba00b42863226cdec3e/libpod/oci_conmon_common.go#L532

And here:
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/da88a5b0977f4387fc60fba00b42863226cdec3e/libpod/oci_conmon_common.go#L599

It seems it is impossible to guareantee that those are gonna arrive to the client side seperatly. 

I also suspect that this thing might be happening to  the `journald` log driver but I was not able to verify it.

What I could offer is to read the reponse body and write it to the `STDOUT` right after handling the response status on the client side.   

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/098f1eb534cbabfc3ca66735a02988a892e13e75/pkg/bindings/containers/attach.go#L141

WDYT? @Luap99

Thank you for the debugging this. I think you are right  about the problem and this likely explains some of the flakes I have been seeing in our CI.

I tried to use the proper 101 upgrade code form the go lib a while ago here https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26594/files. This works for reading and writing but failed for a specifc test when we try to only close stdin as the "EOF" cannot be forwarded that way. Basically there is no way to call CloseWrite() there.

So I think what we can do is combine booth approaches, still hijack the socket net.Conn but use the response.Body for reading/writing the stdio streams.
If that makes sense, I could give it a try implementing your suggestion, though I am not sure if I know the codebase well enough for that task.
If you like that sure, otherwise I can try to finish up the patch later it should be trivial for me. As long as you can test it that would be very welcome.
Ok I updated my patch https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/27061, can you please test it and see if that fully fixes the issue for you
I could verify that the bug is gone with that change, as expected
Would it be possible to backport this change to v5.4.3 so that it is available in the current Debian stable release (trixie)?

No we have no LTS releases and debian most likely would not consume our releases directly anyway as part of the stable repo.
If you need a bug fixed in debian report the bug on the debian bug tracker and link the fix and ask them to backport the patch to the current stable version.
